response_raw:
{
  "mode": "human",
  "topic": "How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.",
  "opinion": "I'm a retired woman living in Los Angeles, and I spend about an hour each day scrolling through Facebook because my grandkids ask me what's happening in the world. Sometimes I see posts that just don't add up—like when someone shares something completely false, or when an advertisement tries to trick you into clicking. It can be frustrating and worrying.

Dealing with fake content isn't easy for someone like me who hasn't had much formal education but knows how to spot a bad deal in real life. My husband used to tell me about that before he passed away; he was good at business things, so maybe I've picked up some of that sensibility even though it's not part of my usual hobby rotation.

One thing that helps is checking if multiple sources say the same thing. If just one person is shouting a story without any proof or links to back them up, it might not be reliable. Also, looking at who posted it—sometimes you can tell by their name and history whether they're credible or not.

I try my best to share only what I know for sure about. And if something feels off, like an article that makes me feel anxious without any real evidence behind it, then I don't post it right away unless I double-check with a trustworthy source.",
  "reasoning_summary": "1. Understanding the user's background (retired woman with limited formal education but high agreeableness and conscientiousness) helps tailor advice to be practical yet grounded.

2. The persona traits indicate low extraversion, which aligns with preferring short social media sessions rather than deep engagement; moderate openness suggests a willingness to learn new ways of handling fake content.

3. High neuroticism requires addressing anxiety about misinformation by providing simple strategies and emphasizing emotional safety through verification steps.",
  "tone": "Calm yet concerned",
  "citations": [],
  "safety_notes": "",
  "used_persona_fields": ["age", "gender", "employment_status", "time_onsocialmedia", "hobbies", "ocean.conscientiousness", "ocean.agreeableness"]

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
I'm a retired woman living in Los Angeles, and I spend about an hour each day scrolling through Facebook because my grandkids ask me what's happening in the world. Sometimes I see posts that just don't add up—like when someone shares something completely false, or when an advertisement tries to trick you into clicking. It can be frustrating and worrying.

Dealing with fake content isn't easy for someone like me who hasn't had much formal education but knows how to spot a bad deal in real life. My husband used to tell me about that before he passed away; he was good at business things, so maybe I've picked up some of that sensibility even though it's not part of my usual hobby rotation.

One thing that helps is checking if multiple sources say the same thing. If just one person is shouting a story without any proof or links to back them up, it might not be reliable. Also, looking at who posted it—sometimes you can tell by their name and history whether they're credible or not.

I try my best to share only what I know for sure about. And if something feels off, like an article that makes me feel anxious without any real evidence behind it, then I don't post it right away unless I double-check with a trustworthy source.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary:
1. Understanding the user's background (retired woman with limited formal education but high agreeableness and conscientiousness) helps tailor advice to be practical yet grounded.

2. The persona traits indicate low extraversion, which aligns with preferring short social media sessions rather than deep engagement; moderate openness suggests a willingness to learn new ways of handling fake content.

3. High neuroticism requires addressing anxiety about misinformation by providing simple strategies and emphasizing emotional safety through verification steps.
